topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box was heavy, smelling faintly of old paper and lavender. I hadn't expected it. Tucked away in the attic, behind a stack of forgotten holiday decorations, sat a cardboard container labeled simply, "Schoolwork - Everything." My fingers, trembling slightly, traced the edges of the tape. Inside, it was a graveyard of completed essays, meticulously drawn diagrams, and neatly folded worksheets from every grade. A slow, cold knot tightened in my chest. For years, I’d felt like a constant disappointment, a failure in every metric my mother seemed to value. Now, seeing this overwhelming evidence of her unseen effort, a sharp, unpleasant sensation rose in my throat. I didn't touch anything, just stood there, the silence of the dusty room amplifying the hollow echo in my own mind.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered, the sound scraping against the stillness of the living room. My mother had always been so meticulous about my grades, so obsessed with perfection. But this—this entire archive of my academic life—it felt like a cruel joke. I picked up a textbook, the spine cracked, and flipped through pages of perfectly rendered notes. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck. I slammed the book shut, the sound sharp and brittle. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent streetlights, feeling a deep, churning resentment bubble up inside me. It wasn't sadness; it was a corrosive feeling, the kind that made the air feel thin and suffocating. I wanted to throw the box across the room, just to see if anything would break.

[story 3]

I sat at the kitchen table, the box spread out before me like a monument to my own perceived shortcomings. Each assignment, from rudimentary arithmetic problems to complex historical analyses, was a testament to hours of unseen labor. I picked up a crumpled piece of paper—a particularly messy science project—and let it fall to the floor. A low, involuntary sigh escaped me, thick with unspoken accusations. I stared at the sheer volume of work, the dedication that had always been directed elsewhere, never towards me. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I pushed the box away with a jerky movement, the scrape of cardboard against the wooden floor sounding deafening in the quiet apartment. I needed distance, needed to scrub this entire situation from my immediate awareness.
